title: The Two Witnesses and the Seventh Trumpet

timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — John Exiled to Patmos

heb.paragraph: John is given a 'measuring rod' (kalamos) and told to 'measure the temple (naos) of God and the altar, and count the worshipers there' (v. 1). The outer court is excluded—'given to the Gentiles' (v. 2). The measuring act draws on Ezekiel 40-48, where a figure measures the eschatological temple, and Zechariah 2:1-5, where Jerusalem is measured as a sign of divine protection and rebuilding. In Revelation's symbolic world, 'measuring' signifies divine preservation and ownership: what is measured belongs to God and is protected. The temple (naos, the inner sanctuary, not hieron, the entire complex) likely represents the church—God's spiritual dwelling place (1 Cor 3:16; 2 Cor 6:16; Eph 2:21)—rather than a rebuilt physical temple in Jerusalem. The measured/unmeasured distinction represents the church's spiritual protection amid physical persecution: believers are preserved spiritually even while exposed to physical harm.

panels.ctx: The measuring of the temple is the final element of the interlude between the sixth and seventh trumpets. Its placement here—after the recommissioning of chapter 10 and before the two witnesses—frames the church's prophetic mission within the context of divine protection. The forty-two months (v. 2) and the 1,260 days (v. 3) are equivalent periods (42 x 30 = 1,260), both drawn from Daniel 7:25 and 12:7, where they represent the duration of the beast's authority over the saints. Whether this period is a literal three-and-a-half years during a future tribulation or a symbolic number representing the entire church age is one of Revelation's major interpretive divides. The half-seven (3.5 years = half of 7) may symbolize an incomplete, bounded period of suffering—intense but not permanent, limited by divine decree.

cross.note: Ezekiel's extensive temple measurement signified God's renewed presence and the restoration of proper worship. In Revelation, measuring signifies divine protection of the worshiping community. What Ezekiel envisioned as a physical temple, Revelation reinterprets as the spiritual community of faith.

Zechariah's vision of measuring Jerusalem was accompanied by the promise that 'I myself will be a wall of fire around it.' Measuring in prophetic literature consistently signifies divine ownership and protection. The measured temple is God's protected space.

The forty-two months correspond to Daniel's 'time, times, and half a time'—the period during which the 'little horn' oppresses the saints before divine intervention. Revelation adopts this period as the duration of both the Gentiles' trampling (11:2) and the witnesses' prophesying (11:3), as well as the woman's wilderness refuge (12:6, 14) and the beast's authority (13:5).

notes.note: The command to measure the temple, altar, and worshipers signifies God's ownership and protection of His people. In Ezekiel's vision (chs. 40-48), the measured temple was the restored eschatological temple. Here, the measuring preserves the true worshipers during the tribulation period. This may refer to a rebuilt temple in Jerusalem during the tribulation, or symbolically to God's spiritual people.

The outer court is 'given to the Gentiles' who will 'trample on the holy city for 42 months.' This trampling echoes Luke 21:24: 'Jerusalem will be trampled on by the Gentiles until the times of the Gentiles are fulfilled.' The forty-two months (three-and-a-half years) corresponds to the second half of Daniel's seventieth week, the period of intense tribulation.

The distinction between the measured sanctuary and the unmeasured outer court teaches us that God's protection of His people is spiritual, not necessarily physical. The church may suffer external persecution (the outer court trampled) while remaining spiritually secure (the inner sanctuary measured and preserved). This is the pattern of the entire church age: outward affliction coexists with inward preservation.

The word naos (inner sanctuary) rather than hieron (whole temple complex) is used throughout Revelation (11:1, 2, 19; 14:15, 17; 15:5, 6, 8; 16:1, 17; 21:22). This is significant because naos denotes the place of God's immediate presence. In 21:22, the New Jerusalem has no naos because God and the Lamb are its temple—the final removal of all barriers between God and His people.

The forty-two months equals 1,260 days (v. 3) equals 'time, times and half a time' (12:14), all equivalent to three-and-a-half years. This period appears six times in Daniel and Revelation, always as the duration of evil's dominion over God's people before divine intervention. It represents a bounded, limited period: half of seven (completeness), signifying that evil's reign is inherently incomplete and temporary.

Beale interprets the temple as the church (1 Cor 3:16-17; Eph 2:19-22), the measuring as divine protection of its spiritual identity, and the trampled outer court as the church's physical vulnerability to persecution. The forty-two months represents the entire church age—the period between Christ's first and second comings during which the church witnesses and suffers. The measured/trampled distinction corresponds to the simultaneously protected and persecuted status of the church throughout history: spiritually secure yet physically exposed.

Osborne notes that the measuring scene creates the theological framework for the two witnesses narrative that follows: the church is both protected (measured) and exposed (outer court trampled). The witnesses' ministry (vv. 3-13) illustrates what this dual status looks like in practice: powerful prophetic witness accompanied by suffering, death, and ultimate vindication. The church is not invulnerable; it is inviolable—not immune to harm but secure in its identity and destiny.

sections.header: Verses 3-14 — "The Two Witnesses: Prophetic Ministry, Martyrdom, and Resurrection"

heb.paragraph: The 'two witnesses' (duo martyres, v. 3) are clothed in sackcloth (mourning and repentance) and prophesy for 1,260 days. Their identity is one of Revelation's most debated questions. Traditional identifications include: Moses and Elijah (based on the miracles of vv. 5-6: fire from the mouth echoes Elijah in 2 Kgs 1:10-12; turning water to blood echoes Moses in Exod 7:17-20); Enoch and Elijah (the two OT figures who did not die, Gen 5:24; 2 Kgs 2:11); or symbolic representatives of the church's prophetic witness. The number 'two' may reflect Deuteronomy 19:15 (two witnesses required for valid testimony) and Zechariah 4:3, 11-14 (the two olive trees identified as 'the two who are anointed to serve the Lord of all the earth'). The term martyres carries its full double meaning: they are both 'witnesses' who testify and 'martyrs' who die for their testimony.

panels.ctx: The two witnesses narrative (vv. 3-13) is one of the most dramatic passages in Revelation. The witnesses exercise extraordinary prophetic power—fire from their mouths, power to stop rain, turning water to blood, striking the earth with plagues—for 1,260 days. Then the beast from the Abyss (its first appearance in Revelation, anticipating chs. 13 and 17) kills them, their bodies lie unburied in 'the great city' (symbolically called 'Sodom and Egypt, where also their Lord was crucified,' v. 8), the inhabitants of the earth celebrate their death (v. 10), and after three and a half days God resurrects them and summons them to heaven in a cloud while their enemies watch (v. 12). A severe earthquake kills seven thousand, and 'the survivors were terrified and gave glory to the God of heaven' (v. 13)—the first positive human response to divine action in Revelation since the seven churches. The narrative encapsulates the church's mission: prophetic witness, persecution, apparent defeat, divine vindication, and resulting repentance. The witnesses' experience mirrors Christ's own: ministry, death, resurrection, ascension.

cross.note: The two witnesses are identified as 'the two olive trees and the two lampstands that stand before the Lord of the earth' (v. 4), a direct quotation of Zechariah 4:3, 11-14. In Zechariah, the two olive trees represent Zerubbabel (royal authority) and Joshua (priestly authority)—the anointed leaders who rebuilt the temple. In Revelation, the witnesses combine royal and priestly functions as the church's prophetic voice.

Elijah's ministry provides the template for the witnesses' powers: shutting the sky so that it does not rain (1 Kgs 17:1) and calling down fire from heaven (2 Kgs 1:10-12). The witnesses exercise Elijah's authority because they stand in Elijah's prophetic succession—calling a rebellious world to repentance.

The power to turn water to blood echoes Moses's first plague against Egypt. The witnesses thus combine the authority of Moses (lawgiver, liberator) and Elijah (prophet, miracle-worker)—the two figures who appeared with Jesus at the Transfiguration (Matt 17:3) and who represent the Law and the Prophets.

notes.note: The two witnesses are most likely Moses and Elijah, returned to earth for a final prophetic ministry during the tribulation. Their miracles correspond to the historical ministries of these two prophets: Elijah stopped rain for three and a half years (Jas 5:17) and called fire from heaven; Moses turned water to blood and struck Egypt with plagues. They prophesy in sackcloth, symbolizing mourning and the call to repentance. Their 1,260-day ministry corresponds to the first half of the tribulation.

When their testimony is complete, the beast from the Abyss (the Antichrist, fully introduced in ch. 13) kills them. Their bodies lie unburied in Jerusalem ('where also their Lord was crucified') for three and a half days while the whole world watches and celebrates. The world's celebration reveals its hatred of God's truth: the only thing the inhabitants of the earth celebrate in the entire book is the silencing of prophetic witness.

After three and a half days, God's breath of life enters them (echoing Ezek 37:5, 10—the dry bones vision), they stand on their feet, and a voice from heaven says 'Come up here.' They ascend in a cloud—the same mode of Christ's ascension (Acts 1:9). A severe earthquake kills seven thousand. The survivors, terrified, 'gave glory to the God of heaven'—the first recorded instance of repentance among the 'inhabitants of the earth.' The witnesses' faithful testimony, sealed by martyrdom and vindicated by resurrection, accomplishes what judgment alone could not.
The second woe (sixth trumpet) is past; the third woe (seventh trumpet) comes quickly.

The witnesses' ministry in sackcloth teaches that prophetic witness is not triumphalist but penitential. They call others to repentance while themselves embodying the posture of repentance. Their extraordinary powers demonstrate that God vindicates His word with supernatural attestation when necessary, but their sackcloth reminds us that prophetic power is exercised in humility, not pride.

The death and resurrection of the witnesses mirrors Christ's own experience: ministry, rejection, death, and vindication. This is the paradigm for the church's mission in every generation: faithful witness will provoke opposition, opposition may lead to apparent defeat, but God will vindicate His people. The world may celebrate the silencing of prophetic voices, but the celebration is always premature—God raises the dead.

This is the first mention of 'the beast that comes up from the Abyss' (to thērion to anabainon ek tēs abyssou), anticipating the fuller description in 13:1-10 and 17:8. The beast is introduced without explanation, suggesting the reader is expected to recognize this figure from Daniel's fourth beast (Dan 7:7-8, 19-25). The beast kills the witnesses only when 'they have finished their testimony' (hotan telesōsin tēn martyrian autōn)—not before. God's servants are inviolable until their mission is complete.

The 'great city' where the witnesses' bodies lie is 'figuratively called Sodom and Egypt, where also their Lord was crucified.' The multiple symbolic names suggest that the 'great city' transcends any single geographical location. It is Jerusalem (where Christ was crucified), but also Sodom (moral depravity, Gen 19) and Egypt (oppression of God's people, Exod 1-15). The city represents the world-system in its hostility to God—the same entity later called 'Babylon' (chs. 17-18).

Beale reads the two witnesses as a corporate symbol of the church's prophetic witness throughout the present age, not as two individual figures in a future tribulation. The number 'two' fulfills the Deuteronomic requirement for valid testimony (Deut 19:15) and echoes Zechariah 4's two olive trees. The witnesses' experiences—powerful testimony, martyrdom, resurrection, ascension—replicate Christ's own pattern and represent the church's mission: to witness faithfully even to the point of death, trusting that God will vindicate. The beast's ability to kill the witnesses only after they 'finish their testimony' demonstrates that the church's mission is sovereign protected until God's appointed purpose is accomplished. The partial repentance of v. 13 ('gave glory to the God of heaven') contrasts sharply with the refusal to repent in 9:20-21, showing that prophetic witness succeeds where judgment alone fails.

Osborne emphasizes that vv. 11-13 provide the most positive response to divine action in the entire book so far. After chapter after chapter of escalating judgment that produces only hardened hearts (9:20-21), the witness-death-resurrection sequence of the two witnesses actually moves some to 'give glory to the God of heaven.' This is Revelation's statement about the theology of mission: the church's most effective witness is not raw power (though the witnesses have extraordinary powers) but faithful testimony sealed by suffering and vindicated by God. The cross-shaped pattern of ministry-death-resurrection is the paradigm for the church's mission, and it alone produces genuine repentance.

sections.header: Verses 15-19 — "The Seventh Trumpet: The Kingdom Proclaimed"

heb.paragraph: The seventh trumpet announces the climactic declaration: 'The kingdom of the world has become the kingdom of our Lord and of his Messiah, and he will reign for ever and ever' (v. 15). The verb egeneto ('has become') is aorist, indicating a completed transfer of sovereignty. In prophetic perspective, the transfer is so certain that it is stated as accomplished fact. This announcement echoes Daniel 2:44 ('In the time of those kings, the God of heaven will set up a kingdom that will never be destroyed') and 7:14, 27 (the Son of Man receives 'an everlasting dominion'). The phrase 'our Lord and of his Messiah' (tou kyriou hēmōn kai tou christou autou) is drawn from Psalm 2:2 (the nations rage against 'the Lord and his anointed'). The seventh trumpet thus announces the fulfillment of the entire messianic hope of the OT.

panels.ctx: The seventh trumpet does not produce a single catastrophic event but rather a heavenly proclamation and worship response that anticipates the end of the story. Like the seventh seal (which opened into the trumpets), the seventh trumpet functions as both climax and transition: it announces the ultimate outcome (God's kingdom established) while setting the stage for the detailed visions that will fill chapters 12-22. The twenty-four elders fall on their faces and worship, celebrating three divine acts: God has 'taken his great power and begun to reign' (v. 17), the nations have raged and God's wrath has come (echoing Ps 2:1-5), and the time has come for judging the dead, rewarding the servants, and 'destroying those who destroy the earth' (v. 18). The opening of God's heavenly temple and the appearance of the ark of the covenant (v. 19) signal that God's covenant faithfulness is the ground of His kingdom. The ark—symbol of God's presence and covenant—was lost when Babylon destroyed the temple in 586 BC; its appearance in the heavenly temple assures believers that what was lost on earth has been preserved in heaven.

cross.note: Daniel's vision of an eternal kingdom that displaces all human empires is fulfilled in the seventh trumpet's announcement. The God of heaven has set up his kingdom, the Son of Man has received his dominion, and the saints of the Most High possess the kingdom forever. The seventh trumpet declares that Daniel's eschatological vision is now reality.

The elders' hymn directly echoes Psalm 2: the nations rage (Ps 2:1), God responds with wrath (Ps 2:5), and his anointed rules with an iron scepter (Ps 2:9). The seventh trumpet is the fulfillment of the royal psalm's vision of messianic sovereignty over all nations.

The ark of the covenant, last seen in Solomon's temple, disappeared when Nebuchadnezzar destroyed Jerusalem in 586 BC. Its reappearance in the heavenly temple (v. 19) signals the restoration of God's full covenant presence—not in an earthly structure but in heaven itself. The covenant that seemed broken is revealed as eternally preserved.

notes.note: The seventh trumpet is the most important announcement in the book: the kingdom of this world has become the kingdom of God and Christ. This is the consummation toward which all of history has been moving. The transfer of sovereignty from human and demonic powers to the rightful King is stated as accomplished fact. Heaven celebrates what earth has not yet fully experienced—the proleptic announcement of the end.

The twenty-four elders' hymn summarizes the entire eschatological program: God has taken His power and begun to reign (the kingdom established), the nations have raged (Ps 2), God's wrath has come (judgment), the dead are judged (final assize), the prophets and saints are rewarded (vindication), and the destroyers of the earth are destroyed (retribution). This is the complete sweep of eschatology in a single hymn.

The opening of God's heavenly temple reveals the ark of the covenant—the symbol of God's presence, His law, His mercy seat, and His covenant faithfulness. The ark has not been seen since the destruction of Solomon's temple. Its appearance in heaven assures believers that God's covenant has never been broken; it has been preserved in the true temple, waiting for its ultimate fulfillment. The lightning, thunder, earthquake, and hailstorm that accompany it signal that God's covenant presence is both glorious and terrifying.

The declaration that the kingdom 'has become' God's kingdom teaches us that what appears to be in the future is already settled in heaven. God's sovereignty is not prospective but actual—it only awaits visible manifestation. The church lives between the announcement and the manifestation, trusting that the heavenly declaration will inevitably become earthly reality.

The appearance of the ark of the covenant is one of the most moving images in Revelation. The ark represents everything Israel lost when Jerusalem fell: God's presence, the tablets of the law, the mercy seat where atonement was made. Its appearance in the heavenly temple says: nothing is truly lost when it is preserved in God's hands. What was destroyed on earth is safe in heaven. The covenant that seemed to end continues forever.

The plural 'loud voices in heaven' (phōnai megalai en tō ouranō) likely includes angels and the redeemed. The singular basileia ('kingdom,' not 'kingdoms') treats the world's political authority as a unified entity now transferred in its entirety to God and Christ. The tense shift from aorist egeneto ('has become') to future basileusei ('he will reign') combines realized and future eschatology: the transfer has occurred; the reign continues forever.

The 'ark of his covenant' (hē kibōtos tēs diathēkēs autou) is the only mention of the ark in the NT. Its appearance in the heavenly temple serves as a transition to chapter 12, where the 'woman clothed with the sun' represents the covenant people of God. The covenant motif bridges the trumpet sequence and the cosmic conflict narrative.

Beale reads the seventh trumpet as the proleptic announcement of the end—not the end itself, but heaven's celebration of the certain outcome. The kingdom transfer is stated in the aorist because from heaven's perspective, it is already accomplished through Christ's death and resurrection. The elders' hymn (vv. 17-18) functions as a table of contents for chapters 12-22: God reigning (chs. 12-14), the nations raging (chs. 12-13), wrath arriving (chs. 15-16), the dead judged (20:11-15), servants rewarded (chs. 21-22), and destroyers destroyed (17-19). The ark's appearance (v. 19) signals that the covenant faithfulness of God is the unifying theme of everything that follows.

Osborne identifies the seventh trumpet as the structural and theological climax of the first half of Revelation. Everything before this point has led to this announcement; everything after it will unpack what it means. The hymn celebrates in advance what chapters 12-22 will depict in detail. This literary technique—announcing the conclusion before narrating the process—creates a hermeneutic of hope: the reader enters the darkest chapters of Revelation (the dragon, the beast, Babylon) already knowing the outcome. The triumph of God's kingdom is not in doubt; only the details of how it unfolds remain to be revealed.

rows.label: vv.1-2
rows.text: Measuring the temple: spiritual protection amid physical exposure

rows.label: vv.3-6
rows.text: Two witnesses: prophetic ministry and supernatural power

rows.label: vv.11-13
rows.text: Resurrection, ascension, earthquake, and partial repentance

rows.label: v.14
rows.text: Second woe past; third woe imminent

rows.label: v.19
rows.text: Heavenly temple opened: ark of the covenant revealed

lit.note: Revelation 11 moves from protective measuring (vv. 1-2) through the dramatic narrative of the two witnesses (vv. 3-13) to the climactic seventh trumpet (vv. 15-19). The chapter bridges the judgment sequences (seals, trumpets) and the cosmic conflict narrative (chs. 12-22). The two witnesses embody the church's mission pattern—witness, persecution, vindication—while the seventh trumpet proclaims the mission's ultimate outcome: God's kingdom established forever.

themes.note: Revelation 11 is rich across nearly all themes. Sovereignty and prophecy dominate (the seventh trumpet announces God's reign; the witnesses embody prophetic mission). Covenant scores high through the ark's appearance. Mission is strong through the witnesses' ministry and the worldwide scope of the seventh trumpet's declaration. Faith appears in the witnesses' perseverance unto death.

hebtext.note: John is given a 'measuring rod' (kalamos) and told to 'measure the temple (naos) of God and the altar, and count the worshipers there' (v. 1). The 'two witnesses' (duo martyres, v. 3) are clothed in sackcloth (mourning and repentance) and prophesy for 1,260 days. Their identity is one of Revelation's most debated questions. Traditional identifications include: Moses and Elijah (based on the miracles of vv. 5-6: fire from the mouth echoes Elijah in 2 Kgs 1:10-12; turning water to blood echoes Moses in Exod 7:17-20); Enoch and Elijah (the two OT figures who did not die, Gen 5:24; 2 Kgs 2:11); or symbolic representatives of the church's prophetic witness. The number 'two' may reflect Deuteronomy 19:15 (two witnesses required for valid testimony) and Zechariah 4:3, 11-14 (the two olive trees identified as 'the two who are anointed to serve the Lord of all the earth'). The term martyres carries its full double meaning: they are both 'witnesses' who testify and 'martyrs' who die for their testimony.

The seventh trumpet announces the climactic declaration: 'The kingdom of the world has become the kingdom of our Lord and of his Messiah, and he will reign for ever and ever' (v. 15). The verb egeneto ('has become') is aorist, indicating a completed transfer of sovereignty. In prophetic perspective, the transfer is so certain that it is stated as accomplished fact. The phrase 'our Lord and of his Messiah' (tou kyriou hēmōn kai tou christou autou) is drawn from Psalm 2:2 (the nations rage against 'the Lord and his anointed'). The seventh trumpet thus announces the fulfillment of the entire messianic hope of the OT.

ppl.name: Moses
ppl.text: Moses stands at the centre of the Pentateuch’s narrative — the one man who spoke with God face to face (Exod 33:11; Num 12:8) and mediated the Sinai covenant to Israel. In Leviticus he receives the priestly legislation; in Numbers he leads, intercedes, and ultimately fails at Meribah (Num 20). The writer of Hebrews calls him "faithful in all God’s house" (Heb 3:5) while distinguishing him from Christ, the Son over the house.

ppl.name: Elijah
ppl.text: The two witnesses are most likely Moses and Elijah, returned to earth for a final prophetic ministry during the tribulation…

trans.title: Comparing translations in The Two Witnesses and the Seventh Trumpet

translations.text: For three and a half days some from every people, tribe, language and nation will gaze on their bodies and refuse them burial.
For three and a half days some from the peoples and tribes and languages and nations will gaze at their dead bodies and refuse to let them be placed in a tomb,
But exclude the outer court; do not measure it, because it has been given to the Gentiles. They will trample on the holy city for 42 months.
but do not measure the court outside the temple; leave that out, for it is given over to the nations, and they will trample the holy city for forty-two months.

Emphasises historical-critical, literary, or text-critical analysis.
Reads The Two Witnesses and the Seventh Trumpet within the canonical framework of fulfilled prophecy and covenant theology.
Represented by Calvin, MacArthur, and the evangelical tradition.
Both readings engage the text seriously; they differ primarily in their hermeneutical starting points and assumptions about authorship and historical context.
